Article.ArticleTitle: Triangulating Instrumental Variable, confounder adjustment and difference-in-difference methods for comparative effectiveness research in observational data.

Abstract.AbstractText: Observational studies play an important role in assessing the comparative effectiveness of competing treatments. In clinical trials the randomization of participants to treatment and control groups generally results in balanced groups with respect to possible confounders, which makes the analysis straightforward. However, when analysing observational data, the potential for unmeasured confounding makes comparing treatment effects more challenging.

Causal inference methods such as Instrumental Variable and Prior Event Rate Ratio approaches enable the estimation of causal effects even in the presence of unmeasured or imperfectly measured confounding factors. Direct confounder adjustment via multivariable regression and propensity score matching also have considerable utility. Each method relies on a different set of assumptions and leverages different aspects of the data.The assumptions of each method are described, and the impact of their violation is assessed in a simulation study. We propose the prior outcome augmented Instrumental Variable method that leverages data from before and after treatment initiation and is robust to key assumption violations. Finally, we propose a heterogeneity statistic to decide if two or more estimates are statistically dissimilar, considering their correlation. We illustrate our framework in an application study assessing the risk of genital infection in type 2 diabetes patients prescribed SGLT2-inhibitors versus DPP4-inhibitors using UK primary care data.

Our proposed approach can estimate treatment effects without bias in scenarios where assumptions of other methods are violated. Furthermore, the application study exemplified the usefulness of discussing the consistency of estimation results from different estimation methods using triangulation.

Triangulating results of different estimation methods is important in observational data to derive high quality evidence. The proposed triangulation framework and heterogeneity statistic are valuable tools to discuss the consistency of estimation results from different methods to shed light on possible sources of bias.

Faculty of Health and Life Sciences, University of Exeter, Exeter, Devon, EX2 5DW, UK.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: Not all research questions and patient populations can be assessed in clinical trials due to ethical issues, costs, and practicality considerations. Therefore, it is important for medical research to derive high-quality real world evidence from observational data, which has been collected for purposes such as administrative tasks and not primarily for research. To assess and compare the effect of treatment options on a specific health outcome of interest from observational data, suitable statistical methods need to be employed to address data quality issues. Ideally, all factors that can simultaneously influence the treatment decision of a clinician and the health outcome of interest need to be included in the analysis to control for their effects and accurately analyse the treatment effect of interest. If some of these factors are not measured in the data, advanced statistical methods need to be employed. We introduce a new method to address this issue and compare it with existing methods. Additionally, we illustrate all methods discussed in a case study for type 2 diabetes research. Our analysis shows that our proposed method is able to provide high quality observational evidence in certain scenarios. Furthermore, the comparison of different methods and our outlined case study provides insights into strengths and weaknesses, of the methods which are important considerations when choosing between them for observational research.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Competing interests: JB is a part time employee of Novo Nordisk. This project is unrelated to his work for the company. The other authors declared there is no conflict of interest.
